_meta.title: Ray Tracing II — Path Tracing, Monte Carlo

questions.question: Why can't a Whitted-style ray tracer produce soft shadows or diffuse interreflection (color bleeding)?
options: It only traces specular (mirror/refraction) rays; diffuse surfaces receive no bounced light except from direct light sources
It uses point lights that are mathematically unable to cast soft shadows regardless of algorithm
It lacks a framebuffer with enough precision to represent gradual shadow transitions
Its shadow rays are limited to one per pixel, which is insufficient for smooth gradients
questions.explanation: Whitted-style ray tracing traces only mirror reflections and refracted rays recursively. When a ray hits a diffuse surface, it just evaluates the direct illumination from point lights and stops — no further bounces. This means diffuse surfaces never sample bounced light from other diffuse surfaces, so color bleeding and soft shadows from area lights are impossible.

questions.question: In Monte Carlo integration, the estimate ∫f(x)dx ≈ (1/N)Σf(xᵢ)/p(xᵢ) converges to the true integral as N→∞. How does the standard deviation of the estimate scale with N?
options: O(1/N) — double the samples, halve the error
O(1/√N) — quadruple the samples, halve the error
O(√N) — error grows with sample count
O(N) — error is proportional to the number of samples
questions.explanation: The central limit theorem tells us that the standard deviation of a Monte Carlo estimator decreases as σ/√N. This means to halve the noise, you need 4× the samples; to reduce it by 10×, you need 100× the samples. This O(1/√N) convergence is independent of dimensionality, which is Monte Carlo's key advantage over deterministic quadrature in high dimensions.

questions.question: In path tracing with Russian roulette, paths are terminated with probability (1 − p_continue) and surviving paths have their contribution divided by p_continue. Why does this keep the estimator unbiased?
options: The random number generator used for roulette is deterministic, so termination doesn't affect expectation
Terminated paths contribute black (zero), which equals the expected value of all paths
The expected value of the surviving contribution remains the same: E[X] = p_continue × E[X/p_continue] + (1−p_continue)×0 = E[X]
Russian roulette is inherently biased; the bias decreases with more samples per pixel
questions.explanation: Russian roulette produces an unbiased estimator because the expected value is preserved. A path either survives (probability p_continue) and contributes X/p_continue, or terminates (probability 1−p_continue) and contributes 0. So E[result] = p_continue × E[X/p_continue] + (1−p_continue) × 0 = E[X]. The variance increases (some paths are lost), but the expectation is correct.

questions.question: Derive the cosine-weighted hemisphere sampling formula: given uniform random r₁,r₂ ∈ [0,1), the direction is d = (√r₁·cos(2πr₂), √r₁·sin(2πr₂), √(1−r₁)). What is the PDF of this direction on the hemisphere?
options: p(ω) = 1/(2π) — uniform over the hemisphere
p(ω) = cos(θ)/π — proportional to the cosine of the polar angle
p(ω) = sin(θ)/π — proportional to the sine of the polar angle
p(ω) = 1/π — constant across all directions
questions.explanation: Cosine-weighted hemisphere sampling generates directions with PDF = cos(θ)/π. This is because in hemisphere coordinates, the area element is sin(θ)dθdφ, and the Jacobian of the mapping from (r₁,r₂) to (θ,φ) with cos(θ)=1−r₁ and φ=2πr₂ yields p(ω)=cos(θ)/π. This PDF is ideal for Lambertian surfaces because the rendering equation's cos(θ) factor cancels with the PDF, simplifying throughput to just the albedo.

questions.question: For a Lambertian (diffuse) surface with BRDF f = ρ/π, when using cosine-weighted hemisphere sampling (PDF = cos(θ)/π), what does the throughput update simplify to at each bounce?
options: throughput *= ρ · cos(θ)² / π
throughput *= ρ / cos(θ)
throughput *= ρ (just the albedo — the BRDF, cosine, and PDF cancel)
throughput *= ρ · cos(θ) / π
questions.explanation: The throughput update is: throughput *= BRDF × cos(θ) / PDF = (ρ/π) × cos(θ) / (cos(θ)/π) = ρ. The π and cos(θ) terms cancel perfectly. This is why cosine-weighted sampling is the natural choice for Lambertian surfaces — it makes each bounce's contribution trivially simple.

questions.question: Which of the following effects emerge naturally from path tracing WITHOUT any special-case code?
options: Only hard shadows and mirror reflections
Caustics, soft shadows, color bleeding, and glossy reflections all emerge from the same Monte Carlo sampling of the rendering equation
Soft shadows and color bleeding require special code; caustics are automatic
Only diffuse interreflection; everything else needs explicit sampling
questions.explanation: Path tracing's elegance is that all these phenomena are just different manifestations of the rendering equation. When a path randomly bounces toward a light source, you get soft shadows (the penumbra is just sampled statistically). When it bounces off a red wall then onto a white floor, you get color bleeding. When it passes through glass, you get caustics. Mirror reflections happen when the BRDF peaks in one direction. No special-case code needed for any of them — though some (like caustics) converge very slowly without dedicated sampling strategies.
